dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won by staying closest to neutral: the main GPT 5.1 discussion was a benchmark thread where commenters mostly rejected the claim that a new open model had truly beaten it [E1], while the only direct negative was a niche request for better compaction/plugin hooks in OpenAI’s CLI tooling [E2].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI had the best day because its direct mentions were limited and relatively contained. The strongest GPT-related thread ended up undermining the challenger rather than piling onto GPT 5.1 [E1], and the other relevant mention was a narrow product-gap complaint about CLI/plugin integration rather than a broad knock on model quality [E2]. That left OpenAI with the highest overall score after balancing sentiment and coverage.

openai.judgementSnippet: GPT 5.1 appeared mainly as the model a rival benchmark claimed to beat, but commenters largely attacked that claim as eval leakage or outright false [E1]; the only direct criticism was a narrow request for better compaction/plugin hooks in OpenAI’s CLI tooling [E2].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: A firsthand report said Claude produced plausible but wrong Spark answers [E3], and another thread wanted Anthropic to expose compaction behavior in Claude Code for plugin authors [E5]; the main offset was skepticism that a 40B open model had really beaten Sonnet 4.5, implying Sonnet was still a high bar [E4].

google_gemini.judgementSnippet: Gemini’s clearest mention was a user saying it gave polished but wrong Spark guidance, with Stack Overflow and official docs ultimately solving the problem instead [E6].

evidence.summary: In the thread about a new open-source code model allegedly beating GPT 5.1, top comments said the eval looked compromised or outright false, which indirectly reinforced GPT 5.1 as a hard benchmark to surpass.
A commenter on the Recursive Language Models story asked OpenAI to expose how compaction works to plugin authors for its CLI tools, signaling a missing product hook.
On the Stack Overflow decline story, a user said Claude was exceptionally good at giving answers that looked reasonable but were wrong for a Spark/Iceberg issue.
The IQuest-Coder benchmark thread drew skepticism that a 40B model had really beaten Claude Sonnet 4.5, with comments pointing to cheating or leakage in the eval.
A commenter wished Anthropic exposed compaction behavior in Claude Code to plugin authors, making the mention a concrete tooling limitation rather than praise.
The clearest Gemini mention was a firsthand complaint that it gave credible-looking but incorrect Spark answers, with Stack Overflow and official docs providing the real fix.
